reviews.review_title: Great combi of beat saber and climbing
reviews.review_description:
I've been playing this game since the beta test.
Although it is EA, you can enjoy a fairly completed game reflecting a lot of feedback over a long period of time. Holding the holders in turn with the hands set to the music's beat seems simple, but there are many obstacles, such as speeding up the beat in the middle and having to twist the holders in a set direction. There are even holders that you have to jump to hold like rock climbing, and if you lose both hands in a panic, you fall to the floor and it's hard to regain the rhythm and position.
Please get the hang of it at a low level and try to reach a high level. It's still hard for me to get to the end above the intermediate level.
It's not just fitness, but it's a great app that allows you to exercise and build agility to some extent.
There are not many titles of songs yet, and there are no popular songs of various genres, but I look forward to gradually expanding.

reviews.review_title: Climbing + Rhythm = match made in heaven
reviews.review_description:
I am personally in love with rhythm games, and I think that climbing is one of the funnest things in VR. This game is such a fantastic fusion! There are plenty of personal settings to adjust latency, which is a must for me in a rhythm game, and there are a bunch of fun mechanics like twist grip and downward dynos that feel insanely satisfying when you drop in to the beat of some fun tunes! 

I would like more places/arenas to climb, and more content in general for its longevity, but it is new for now and what is present is fantastic!

developer_response.body: Hey chickensink, thank you for the feedback! We'll note down your suggestions for more arenas to climb in!

reviews.review_description:
(product received for free)
This one is tough as I like rhythm games like Ragnarock. I love climbing in VR! I thought it was going to be a slam dunk. For me though it just didn't quite hit right so maybe watch some gameplay or play the demo before deciding.

The climbing and grabbing felt great. 10/10 there. very snappy and responsive. Physics of grabbing, pulling, falling, and jumping all felt wonderful. The environment was fine and the hand holds were easy to see. It has a well-done tutorial to give you all the enhanced mechanics that turn climbing into a rhythm game. That is where for me the mix of two things I loved just didn't turn out how I expected. 

It was well made. Gameplay made sense and the music synced well with actions. I will rate it a 3 since it is a well-made game with solid mechanics, it just didn't click with me.

developer_response.body: Hey Nephilim42, thank you for taking the time to leave us a review. We understand that the game may not be for everyone, but we appreciate you giving us a chance!

reviews.review_title: Really nice combo of rhythm and climbing
reviews.review_description:
Refreshing new take on rhythm games by making you climb, twist grip, drop and jump in time to the music. Feels great when you get into the flow zone.
Good choices of difficulty levels but would be nice with a bit more variety in type of songs.
Developer seems very open to feedback and patches have been good, looking forward to future updates.

reviews.review_title: A fresh new ryrhm game w excellent music
reviews.review_description:
I love VR climbing (eg The Climb 1/2, Population One) and I love rhythm games (eg beat saber, O Shape, Pistolwhip) so this game caught my attention immediately.
The mechanics are spot on and the difficulty rating lets the player learn them one by one without feeling overwhelmed. I've only tried up to level 3 and what I've seen, alone, already makes the levels super interesting.
The songs are very good as well, some of them seem better suited for some of the mechanics (eg the rotating grips that break the glass) but they all work well.
So overall this is an excellent title to buy if you like VR rhythm games.
In terms of improvements, I supposed it would be nice to have more songs but I'd focus my attention on getting the game to run as smoothly as possible. There's a slight stutter that becomes annoying when playing at the higher difficulty levels. It makes the game enjoyable at <4 levels but kills my interest to attempt higher difficulty or nailing perfect hit scores.

developer_response.body:
Hey Jadranko.K-K, thank you for your feedback! We are working on improving the game performance as best as we can. 

PS. Amazing work on the leaderboards, we see your name pop up everywhere!

reviews.review_description: Didn’t expect this game to be so fun. It combines to of my favorite VR style games, climbing and rhythm. Level and sound design is really good too. Would definitely recommend!

reviews.review_title: Fresh take on VR rhythm games
reviews.review_description: A fun unique premise! A wonderful rhythm game that involves rock climbing elements including swing and dropping, all on the beat. Very nice especially when I got into the flow of things. Not precisely a workout game though my shoulders were certainly feeling it after a while. With the addition of an editor to create your own beat maps, I think this game has some legs!

I didn't realize how much I don't do until I played this.  My body seems to have evolved into doing what it needs to mode, and after playing this, I wonder if I choose to not do things that have inadvertently inhoboted my range of mnotion.  This game was hard for me.  It was a wakeup call that I have needed for some time.  Thankfully, no matter if I tierd out it didn't boot me off the level, and that's what brings me back.  I find this allows me to avoid being self conscious since I do not fully realize how well I went by game standards until the song completed.  Having multiplayer options adds more opportunity for players who want to add another level to their game play.  The music is great and there's a 'local custom songs' feature I am excited to learn more about.  This game is great for building coordination skills and I suspect it will improve strength during those slower movements where iit feels like you're hanging....hanging...hanging....I also don't mind when I fall down, because the scramble up is a lot more fun than craling along the floor.  When the game gets ahead of your stamina, you can catch it up.  Most games just fail you and make you start over.  That's not been my experience Peak Rhythm.

developer_response.body: Hey intjmega, it's so great to hear that the game has been helping you with bettering your overall health and hopefully it can continue to help you with improving your range of motion one song at a time! The most important thing is to not strain yourself and to take it slowly. All the best, take care, and have fun!

reviews.review_description: Game is fun climbing to the beat, it isn't too strenuous but seems to be a good workout anyway. The music is good and the visuals are nice. Would recommend to anyone looking for a bear saber alternative

So I have many active fitness apps and games in VR and am always looking for a new great challenge. I would have to say that this is actually one of the more fun and enjoyable challenges I've had, even when it was in the a beta test phase I was a part of. It had some bugs but was still effective from the start.

So picture a blank rock climbing wall and as the music begins one of the handholds shows up in front of you. It 's a holographic hand hold that becomes solid as you grab it in sync with the musical beat. Then, after you've grabbed that one, light beam travels from that to another location on the wall where there's a new holographic hand hold. And as the light beam hits that on the musical beat, you are expected to grab that with either your left or your right hand depending on whether or not you have a green light ray or a pink light ray. There's also a blue handhold that appears that you are supposed to grab with both hands. Some have you twist them as you grab them as well.

So basically that's how this progresses throughout the song. These handholds will appear in different locations and you grab them as soon as that beam hits them when aligned with the beat of the music. Every time you miss or grab off the beat, you get a big old "X"on it to show you didn't grab it at the correct time. There are also some that appear below you where you have to strategically let your hands go and drop down to that particular hand hold and grab it as your hand lands on it. If you have faster music or the beat speeds up, then of course, you've got to cross your arms over one another and move around as quickly as you can.

This definitely provides a great physical workout, but it is also really great for your mind as well because you have to be "in the zone", so to speak. You have to focus to really be able to follow where you need to go, especially with the increase in difficulty level. I break a sweat pretty quickly. It is definitely not as easy as it sounds.Especially as you begin to increase difficulty levels.

The one thing I will caution people about is if you have any issues with upper body injuries or handicaps, especially with regard to shoulders. This really does work your arms. But your shoulders get an awful lot of the workout. Just make sure this wouldn't exacerbate anything that you've already got going on, just as you would with any fitness activity or game.

Overall, I would say that this is definitely an app I'm going to be recommending to my friends. I mean an app where you can work your mind-body connection, break a sweat and continuously challenge yourself, is a fantastic way to spend some time in VR.

I love climbing, and I love rhythm games. The moment I learned about this game I had it on my radar. Overall, I feel like they're onto something that can be great. 

The climbing feels good, VR controls are responsive, and there is some variety. However, my biggest gripe is I wish there was more variety. There are several different input "methods" when climbing (grabbing, twisting, dropping) which is great. The environment, and especially music, is lacking heavily in variety, in my opinion. I do hope as the game grows and evolves, that variety is the primary focus. The climbing mechanics are fun, the base is absolutely there. 

The difficulty curve feels good for the first few difficulty bumps, but then becomes too difficult too quickly, for me. I'm not some master at rhythm games, so maybe this feels better for those that are better at these games, but it felt like I ran into a brick wall. Which was fine, I still had fun with the lower levels, but things got too intense for me. It feels like just a frantic dance for me, rather than a climbing game. 

Those two gripes aside, I highly recommend the game if it looks interesting to you. It's in EA, but the potential feels like it's there to go from good to great.

reviews.review_title: Great new take for a rhythm game
reviews.review_description:
Peak Rhythm is a great new take on a VR rhythm game, with some catchy songs. As you move on through difficulty levels the actions you need to do start increasing, from twists, jumps, drops, double grabs.

 As you play through the game you start getting into a flow state especially when you find a difficulty and song the really clicks with you. 

Definitely can get you sweating if you are on high difficulty songs, but overall I definitely enjoyed my time playing it!

A fresh and unique take on rhythm games, blending music with climbing mechanics in a really satisfying way. When everything syncs up, it creates a smooth, immersive flow that feels great—especially when played standing.

The difficulty is challenging but rewarding, though the game is still in early access and could use more variety in its music selection. That said, it shows a lot of promise, especially with future content and custom tracks.

Overall, a fun and innovative rhythm experience that’s definitely worth trying.

reviews.review_title: Great premise for a VR game
reviews.review_description: This is a pretty great idea for a game. It's a mix of beat saber with climbing. On paper, it seems like a slam dunk. From a gameplay perspective, it's got good ideas, but the mechanics of the game make it kind of tricky to blend the music with the rhythm of climbing. You either focus on the climbing, or you focus on the music, but the two don't mesh as well as you'd expect with the way the game is structured. You never get that feel of hitting a beat when you grab or twist or leap or drop and catch a ledge. That being said, it's a great workout and your shoulders will start to feel it after just a short while. Even without the music blend, the gameplay really makes up for it. This will keep you busy for awhile, as long as your shoulders hold up.

developer_response.body: Hey Kwijgaming, thank you for the feedback regarding the music & rhythm of climbing. We appreciate it and we're glad to hear that outside of that, you are still having a good time!

reviews.review_title: A new take on a rhythm game
reviews.review_description:
I genuinely enjoyed my time with this, even with a few rough edges. If I had to describe the vibe, imagine Beat Saber, but instead of slashing blocks with swords, you're aggressively grabbing wall sockets. It feels a lot like indoor rock climbing, except the grips are moving to the beat and you have to twist them, drop, and move with the flow.
Here are my main takeaways after playing:

- The Flow State is Real: On paper, rhythm + climbing sounds tricky to pull off, but when it clicks, it really clicks. You aren't just holding on; you’re doing double grabs, twisting the holds, and doing these wild jumps to catch the next ledge. When the mechanics sync up perfectly with the rhythm, it feels incredibly smooth and satisfying.

- The Music is Mid: Being totally honest, the built-in soundtrack just wasn't for me. It’s a very specific mix of EDM, dubstep, and drum & bass, and since the game is still in Early Access, there are only about 7 tracks right now. It desperately needs more genre variety (some metal or rock would be awesome). Luckily, there is a custom beatmap editor, which is a massive saving grace for future content.

- Shoulder Burner: Do not sleep on the physical aspect. Your shoulders will feel it after just a short session. It’s a fantastic way to warm up your reflexes and get a quick sweat in, especially if you play standing up.

- Early Access Quirks: Because it's such a new concept, it has some growing pains. The difficulty curve is a bit all over the place right now (jumping randomly from levels 1, to 3, to 9). The early levels feel painfully slow, but on the absolute highest expert difficulties, it kind of loses the "climbing" feel and devolves into a frantic tap-fest where hand-detection can sometimes pull you to the wrong grip.

- The Environment: One thing to note is that you aren't actually scaling a massive mountain environment. You're basically on a hexagonal elevator platform moving up and down to the beat.

If you like rhythm games, you're going to want to check this out. It has climbing mechanics, but it's not really a game where you scale a mountain. You'll grab spots that appear to music. Sometimes you'll grab multiple times the same spot and other times you'll twist. The jumps and drops are wild, especially when moving at pace or a higher difficulty. This game will definitely warm up your reflexes.

This is a unique and well executed game in the rythem genre. You are using hand motion and movements to the beat while escalating higher and faster up a climbing wall. The idea is to grab, twist, or pull your way up while syncing to the beat of some very electric and entertaining music. You really feel that beat when you hit your mark and catch on to the groove of the song. There are multiple soundtracks as well as difficulty levels to choose from.
I work on my feet all day and don't get a lot of upper body use; this game is my goto on days off to help strengthen my upper body.  I definitely recommend this for anyone in a similar situation,  or perhaps if you are, for example wheelchair bound.

reviews.review_title: A unique twist on the format
reviews.review_description: This vr rhythm game plays a lot more differently then other vr rhythm games and that’s what so great about it. The gameplay is a lot more focused on timing of your hits then other games like beat saber & synth rider where you mainly care about combo and accuracy. Once you get used to this gameplay change everything starts flowing together with this catchy original soundtrack. If you like vr rhythm games I highly recommend checking this one out.

developer_response.body: Hey Zeenaught, thank you for the kind review!

reviews.review_description:
I approve of this game, I have never seen this combination of rhythm and climbing anywhere, it also has a good execution of this idea.

The music is pretty good, and there’s a low skill floor and a high skill ceiling which I approve of

8/10, this game is worth your time

developer_response.body: Hey 01arcanum, glad to hear that you liked the combo! Thank you for playing our game.

reviews.review_description:
peak rhythm is a unique take on the vr rhythm game genre with its main premise being climbing a building to the beat of the music, there are multiple mechanics in this game like jumping long distance from one block to another, or grabbing it with both hands and plenty more, there is a selection of 7 songs of the genre being electronic dance music/dubstep/drum and bass, with these type of genres being one of my favourite, 

I would naturally gravitate to it giving it a higher score, however one thing that is confusing is the difficulty rating on it being 1 to 10 however not every song has a 1 or 10 like the beginning songs only have 3 difficulty ratings being 1,3 and 9 but the last song has 3,5 and 10, making the difficulty harder the later the songs are in the playlist, however I feel like for some songs the level 7 is more difficult than a level 9 for example, so I feel like ditching the number rating and just making it easy, medium and hard so there wouldn't be any confusion.

And for the difficulty of each song, well it starts of fine with the begging songs feeling like a breeze and the 4-6 seems to be a step up however the late game difficulties is just hard to keep up with, with it being very random like going from the left hand to left hand up and than straight back down with it being hard to follow and having to do the stage multiple times just to know where it will go next, I wish with future songs or remakes of older songs it could be more predictable of where the notes will be and easier to keep up with, or it might just be that I am not good at expert level songs in the rhythm genre.

I would recommend this game for some one who is already interested in rhythm games or is new to it who loves unique take on rhythm games and wouldn't mind on how difficult the later songs become.

reviews.review_title: An original and fresh rhythm game !
reviews.review_description:
P.Rythm is a music video game that attempts to stand out in this highly competitive genre by combining rhythm and climbing.
The game is accessible to everyone (standing/sitting) and doesn't cause motion sickess.

The game currently consists of 7 tracks (each with 3 difficulty levels).
Musically, it's quite eclectic : you'll either love it or hate it.

Graphically, the game is quite simple and clean.
Each level follows the same structure/scenery : you start on a platform and ascend not using your climbing skills, but by using a hexagonal elevator platform that you grip onto and perform climbing moves according to a predefined pattern inside it.
Which is unfortunate because it gives the impression of moving around in a square.

The game has numerous options for customizing your experience. You'll also have detailed stats on your run to help you improve. 
There's a leaderboard too.

Regarding the gameplay itself :
Each hand is represented by a color and you should follow the pattern shown on the screen.
You'll encounter climbing mechanics introduced progressively throughout the levels : holding a hold with two hands, letting go to land lower, rotating a hold, jumping, etc...
Failure isn't very punishing (a good thing) because you can always recover from a missed sequence.
Paradoxically, the more the difficulty increases, the less you feel like you're playing a climbing game and at the maximum difficulty level, it becomes a pure tap-tap rhythm game where reflexes take precedence. The visibility is a problem too.
The game is not a workout either and you will never break a sweat, so don't buy it for that.

The foundations are there and quite solid (broadly reminds me quite a bit of a "budget" Beat Saber) but the balance between climbing and rhythm game is a little unbalanced. Visually, I feel like something's missing too.
I am also surprised by the responsiveness, attentiveness and desire of the studio to do well. This is really noticeable (hence the more lenient rating).

developer_response.body: Hey ff7clad, thank you for the detailed review and for playing during our early access launch. We very much appreciate you giving attention to the accessibility of the game (eg. seated play, and no motion sickness). Right now, we're working on polishing the game further based on feedback we receive and can't wait to show you all more of what we have in store!

reviews.review_description: Very seldom do i feel like a VR game truly takes pre-existing ideas and truly makes something unique through their execution and combination. Case in point, Peak Rhythm. Fusing rhythm and climbing, two genres of VR game that have been done in many ways, but rarely done together, and rarely this well. While its execution isn't pitch perfect, so far it proves its potential. I would definitely suggest this to a new VR gamer since its all intuitive, and the tutorial was effective. I cant wait to watch this game grow as it should.

This is a new type of rhythm game that incorporates climbing. The music is catchy and the climbing mechanics are simple. As the levels increase the difficulty and speed also increases. Everything is explained in a clear way in the tutorial , which gives you plenty of time to practice before heading into the game. I can see this becoming very addictive and the need to beat each level like beat sabre. You start on the lower floor and climb your way upwards following the beat of the current song. At times it’s straight climbing then twists of the hand grab and even dropping or pulling yourself to the next hand hold. There’s a great mix to keep you interested .

I am an older player and not in the best of health. I found playing the game challenging but I was able to play in short bursts. I can see myself using this game to help me get fit and the more I play the longer I can build up the amount of time I’ll be able to play for.

developer_response.body: Hey Lexiee1, thank you for the positive review. It's good to hear that the tutorial came across clear, and it's super cool to know that Peak Rhythm is helping you to stay fit! We're currently still working on polishing the game further, with plans to release more content later down the line. Thank you for playing Peak Rhythm!

reviews.review_title: Climbing meets rhythm — and it actually
reviews.review_description: A genuinely original idea that pulls off something I didn't expect — rock climbing and rhythm game mechanics blend together surprisingly well, and once you find your groove, the flow state is real. The soundtrack covers drum & bass, dubstep, and house and fits the vibe perfectly. Being Early Access, the track count is still limited, but the custom beatmap editor is a nice bonus. Will definitely be coming back once more content drops.

developer_response.body: Hey Cy80r6, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our  Early Access phase! We're working to bring you more songs to climb to, so keep an eye out for future content drops. We appreciate your enthusiasm!

reviews.review_description:
This game is a unique kind of rhythm game that I didn't expect to enjoy very much, but I truly did!

This game certainly has areas for improvement but is overall quite good. I believe it truly requires a wider selection of songs. A few metal songs would be awesome lol, but regardless, a mix would be fantastic.

I discovered that this game was quite simple to pick up and realized it was very clear when you struck the notes accurately. It gave you a sense of accomplishment.

In general, I would really enjoy playing this again in the future

But its hard to recommend where it is now. The early levels are painfully slow, but that's understandable. Fun starts with jumps, so around difficulty 4+, but i don't think the devs understand jumps the same way - for me it's fun to launch myself with one hand, to catch a grip with the other. What appears more often is let go with f.e. right, and then catch with right again, which allows for way less precision.
 And precision is important, since detection at high speeds is quite bad, often pulling your hand to a grip you didn't mean to catch.

Because of the whole "moving up and down" akin to bouldering, instead of it being actually climbing up, it feels like I'm playing a mime instead, and I can't shake off that feeling...

The game has a very narrow sweetspot, where it's genuinely fun, but everything easier is too slow, and everything harder is just annoying because of the two issues in the first paragraph.

developer_response.body:
Hey Ishi112, thank you for the feedback. Regarding the earlier levels, we wanted to provide a range for new players to familiarize themselves with the way the game is played outside of the tutorial before ramping up the difficulty. For some people, that may be diving headfirst into difficulty 3, instead of 1 or vice versa. It's impressive that you're already up to difficulty 4+! 

We're still working on polishing the game further and we appreciate the feedback that you've provided here, especially on the jumps, and the feeling of climbing. You've given us some things to think about. Thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access!

reviews.review_title: New spin on rhythm games, w/ some issues
reviews.review_description:
I have played quite a few rhythm games over my years, and Peak Rhythm definitely stands out as unique.

The basic gist of the gameplay is like a rock climbing wall meets the game "bop it", or maybe "whack a mole". You're on a virtual climbing wall, and grips appear and dissappear one by one in rhythmic patterns for you to "grab to the music". 

There are a few different type of grips/targets that appear, all with different moves. Grips are colour-coded to left hand, right hand, or two-hand. There are grips where you have to let go and drop to grab another grip, jump grips where you have to fling yourself, and twist grips with blades on the side where you have to twist them back and forth to the music.

It's a novel twist on the rhythm game formula. There are a handful of electronic tunes (seven songs, currently, each with three difficulties), and the game is very colourful. I enjoyed the easy/medium difficulties, but the harder difficulties are not at all well balanced IMO. The choreography and difficulty balancing absolutely need to be reworked for the harder difficulties, as I found it nearly impossible to follow. I also found the jump mechanic to often be frustrating, but the rest of the mechanics such as drop and twist felt good. This IS an early access title, so all of this stuff can still be improved. The dev team is also working on a feature where users can import custom songs, though I don't know how that'll work or when it's coming.

I should note that I playtested the game, so I got to play it for free. I tend to prefer rhythm games that are more fitness-focused, but Peak Rhythm's novel design will make it stand out for many users. The devs also enable you to provide ratings/feedback on the music, choreography and overall fun after you complete each track/level, so it's clear the devs are eager to respond to input. I find the early access version to be a significant improvement  over the playtest build, so I'm sure it'll keep on improving.

Its nice to see a VR rythm game coming out that isnt yet another beat saber clone with a twist. Its more themed around climbing, though its not exactly climbing a path but more back and forth on a wall. The mechanics are interesting and serve for a nice flow. Missing a note can be a bit punishing tho as catching up or getting back to the right color is really difficult. Speaking of difficulty, it definitely has something for the players that really wanna dig in and challenge themselves. Just the song collection could have been bigger

developer_response.body: Hey gWinnieVR, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We're still working to polish the game further, and there are plans to release more songs later down the line so stay tuned for that!

reviews.review_description:
Peak Rhythm is a refreshing twist on the rhythm genre, turning music gameplay into a climbing challenge where you grab, twist, and move between holds in time with the beat. Notes are color-coded so you must plan which hand to use, and the game introduces clever mechanics like fling notes (requiring quick same-hand grabs), drop notes that make you fall and catch the next hold mid-descent, multi-grabs usable with either hand, and twist holds that must be rotated in rhythm. The songs are catchy, and the mechanics create a satisfying blend of rhythm, coordination, and physical movement that doubles as a surprisingly good workout. It feels a lot like real life “Bop It” but harder.
Overall, Peak Rhythm already offers a unique rhythm experience that engages both your mind and your body. If you enjoy rhythm games and want something that gets you moving, it’s absolutely worth trying while it grows toward its full release.

developer_response.body: Hey FoamyMouthDog, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We're happy to hear that the core mechanics is unique and we are working on expanding the game further!

reviews.review_description:
With a stronger UI, and some inspired modders to chart some songs, there's potential for fun. In the meantime, I think the release is too early.

As a musician, this is like sight reading a brand new piece of music but you're only able to see the next note, not two or three, just next. I get it, it's all about finding flow.

The game does its best to give you enough cues to prepare for what's next, but the song charts ramp up the difficulty with inconsistent patterns, endless hand crossovers and major shifts in speed which made the experience fall short of finding my groove at anything above the base difficulty.

I also wasn't able to perfect any songs. The sweet-spot for a perfect note comes early IMO. I can hear the clack on my controllers before the game registers a hit. Fixable, but very tedious to do in the options menu. You have no visual indicator what the settings and their numbers do. You'll have to replay a song many times to understand what you've changed. My main suggestion is to put some visual feedback in the settings menu so I can see what I'm adjusting. And for the love of everything VR upgrade from the impossibly small font and tiny buttons that only exist in the settings.

developer_response.body: Hey SkateLemonade, thank you for taking the time to lay out your thoughts. You have a solid point with the UI and we're aware that there are some rough edges that needs to be polished. As we work on expanding the game, it's our intention to iron out these things. We appreciate you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access!

reviews.review_description: This game combines rock wall climbing and music, the hand holds are colored for each hand where you climb to the beat of the music.  The climbing mechanic feels great and some of the hand holds you have to twist to reveal the next one in the path, you also in later levels have ones where you need to drop and catch it or fling yourself up to grab it.  Timing your grab of the hand hold is key to scoring better, there is a visual queue to time your grab and you get visual feedback if the grab was perfect, or a miss.  When you complete your song you can look down and see how far you have climbed.  This is early access there currently arent a lot of songs but each song has multiple levels of difficulty and the roadmap shows multiplayer and more songs on the way, there is also custom songs but i haven't tried that yet.  This was fun and great for arm day workouts.

developer_response.body: Hey Ellehcim99, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm in Early Access! We're happy to hear that the game is giving you quite the work out. Rest assured, increasing the song list is definitely a priority for us as we work on expanding the game, so stay tuned!

reviews.review_description:
This was a nice change of pace to most rythem games.  The game really keeps you on your toes.  The wall climbing is fun and exciting though on the highest difficulties it gets hard to keep up with the speed and change ups.  While it is a learning curb to do well it was pretty easy in the middle difficulties to get into a flow.  
  The songs were great and worked well with the mapping on most.  While the editor is a great addition the game will really shine once some decent mappers join the community.

developer_response.body: Hey TbcVRgaming, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We're happy to hear that the core mechanics are fun and exciting to you and we're working on more behind the scenes!

reviews.review_description:
As a long‑time rhythm gamer, Peak Rhythm instantly grabbed my attention — and honestly, it’s one of the freshest takes on the genre I’ve played in VR. When the beat lines up and the movement flows, the game delivers that addictive “locked‑in” feeling every rhythm fan chases. The climbing mixed with musical timing is such a clever twist, and the neon visuals amplify the energy in all the right ways.

Yes, it’s still Early Access, and you can feel that in a few spots. Some grabs don’t register the way I expect, and a handful of rhythm cues land slightly off‑sync. But even with those rough edges, the core gameplay loop is already fun and surprisingly satisfying. You can tell there’s a strong foundation here, and I kept wanting to replay tracks just to chase cleaner lines and smoother climbs.

If you love rhythm games and enjoy mastering flow, timing, and movement, Peak Rhythm is absolutely worth jumping into. It’s stylish, it’s unique, and it has huge potential as it continues to grow. I’m excited to see how far this one goes.

developer_response.body: Hey Zyphr2099, thank you so much for playing Peak Rhythm in early access! We're working to polish the game further and we're glad to hear that the core gameplay loop translated well. Excited to show you more of what we have in store further down the line!

reviews.review_title: A fresh take on Rhythm Games
reviews.review_description: This game is in essence a mix of almost a fitness game and a rhythm game. It will get you moving. There is also a tool coming for custom mapping of new tracks so the content will be there for you to try. If you like climbing games but want to also listen to some music while you do this is perfect for you. It can also be played while sitting but I think standing is definitely a better option, but having options is good. The selection of tracks has a bit for everyone so it's nice to keep advancing and checking out new tracks.

reviews.review_title: A different kind of rhythm game
reviews.review_description: very different from most of the rhythm games out. instead of looking ahead and having notes come to you your on a climbing wall looking around yourself to see the next handhold you need to grab. while the easy difficulty is fun and the mapping is quite good to the songs. the harder difficulty mechanics can easily mess you up mainly the jumping can catching with the same hand. also when you fail to grab the next point the game feels very punishing cause you can fall of the wall and have to climb back up with out the song pausing. but you only really fall on the jumps so if they can update that mechanic it'd be a fun different rhythm game.

When i first heard its a rhythm climbing, i imagined a combination of the climb but with music, epic jump from building to building, ect however in this gane i feel like climbing up and down a 4mx4m wall rather than a building and cliff. Since i am always facing a wall i barely notice the machine i am on is  climbing up a building. Lastly, to access more/higher levels you are forced to play 1 of the 2 lv 1 songs. With the slow tempo, my arm is aching holding up.. But that might just be a childhood injury acting up preventing me to lift my arm for too long.

developer_response.body: Hey ZnightS, thank you for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We appreciate your feedback regarding the feeling of climbing and we're working to further expand and polish the game. We wanted to make sure players have time to get used to the movement and to know how to expect the next grip, which is why we wanted new players to start with level 1s. If you readjust the grip position to the middle of your chest after you grab them, it should help ease the aching so you don't have to hold your hand up until it's time to grab the next hold.

reviews.review_description:
Overall, I think this game has been a great way to get some exercise, as it gives satisfying feedback. Though given it's EA status, expect placeholder graphics (Still fine currently) and not too wide selection of songs. Despite this, I'd highly recommend giving it a try as it gives a unique way to play a rhythm game
---
* Entanglement of both mind and body.
More so an issue on the harder levels. But I've found the sequences that require you to quickly use the same arm, or switch direction with the other arm uncomfortable to do. 
 This is mainly because I've found readability/contrast not to be good enough to quickly analyze what do next. Some fixes I would recommend would be emphasizing when a puck requires the same hand, and possibly having a 45degree min if crossing arms is necessary/less horizontal passes. Still, mostly an issue with harder levels, as I've found myself enjoying the levels more to my pace!
Another point: I've also found recovery hard on the more difficult levels. As trying to quickly reattach has found my grabbing the wall instead of the puck. Possibly a skill issue, but it just doesn't feel right/as if that's what I did
---
* Something to emphasize the "climbing" aspect. 
 I've never really noticed the change in elevation during gameplay, and have only realized it at the end. 
 Not too big of an issue, especially since I'd like the gameplay problems I listed before to be improved. Just something I'd hope to see upon full release 
---
* Personally I'd love for "perfects" to get more leniency.
 They really do give satisfying feedback, but I've personally found it a bit too stingy on the timing. I don't mind it giving the same score despite being considered perfect, but do please give me more of that sparkle!
 Or suppose you can atleast give lesser effects towards goods/almost perfects
---
And with that, I hope you all the best with early access. This game certainly has the potential, and I can't wait to see how far it'll come once fully released!

developer_response.body: Hey DoodleGangee, thank you for taking the time to lay out your thoughts! We're aware that there are some rough edges to polish, but we're glad to hear that the core aspects of the game already feels promising. As for the Drops and one-handed Jumps, those are definitely mechanics that will take a bit of practice, but we hear you and we’re working to smooth out the learning curve during Early Access with more lower-difficulty maps.

reviews.review_title: Peak Rhythm: Music and Climbing Collide
reviews.review_description:
Peak Rhythm is a really cool twist on rhythm games. Instead of just hitting notes, you're actually climbing while moving to the beat of the music. Once you get into the rhythm, it feels really smooth and satisfying as you climb higher through each level.

The game is full of bright colors and energetic music that keep things fun and engaging. Different motions are introduced as you progress, so it’s not just repeating the same movement over and over. You have to grab, twist, drop, or jump with the correct hand while still doing it to the beat of the music. You really have to pay attention to the rhythm if you want to score well.

What surprised me most was how quickly the challenge ramps up. It starts off pretty simple, but as you move through the levels the timing and movements become more demanding, which makes it really rewarding when you nail a section perfectly or close to perfect.

If you like rhythm games and want something a little more active and different, this is definitely worth checking out. It’s a fun mix of music, movement, and climbing that keeps you coming back to try to beat your score or climb the leaderboard.

This is especially great for players who enjoy rhythm games like Beat Saber but want something that involves more movement and climbing.

This is currently in Early Access, so there will be more added later, including a multiplayer mode. Can’t wait to see what new features the developers add next, it’s already a blast!

Had this game since early access and it has come quite a way. Peak Rhythm has you climbing, and dropping to the beat of music. And sometimes making a leap of faith. The tunes are catchy, the graphics are solid, and the workout factor is a high. If you are looking to add to your VR workout repertoire, highly recommend you give this game a look.

developer_response.body: Thanks for the review and for playing Peak Rhythm since our earlier testing! It means a lot to hear you’ve seen the game come a long way, and we appreciate all the feedback. We’re glad the mix of climbing, drops, jumps, catchy music, and physical intensity is coming together in a fun and satisfying way. It’s also great to hear the game feels like a solid addition to a VR workout lineup. Thanks again for the support and recommendation!

reviews.review_description: I received a copy for testing, but I honestly could see myself paying full price for this game.  I couldn't fully get down the high-difficulty throws but I see the vision, and I do think it's doable.  The visuals and haptics give you a good idea of when to grab, so even when I feel like it's not 100% matching the timing I expect, I can see when they want me to be grabbing and can do well.  Clever ideas on the various mechanics, like the twisting blade handhold, make this feel unique and fun.  The only issues I could come up with are really minor gripes that could just be me, so I think this is a solid 5-star and easy recommendation.

I'm so happy this game has come out.  I love music and rhythm games but they are becoming very similar to each other.  This one has not.  It combines climbing mechanics with musical beats and does it in a fun unique way.  It definitely gets you into a flow state feeling.  Music is upbeat and catchy.  The visuals and environments are pretty basic but it gets the job done and not really needed much improvement when you are moving about on the climbing wall.  It did feel like a nice workout and I my arms got tired and were sore the next day.  I love that a custom creation mode is included to add to the value of the game.  I hope new songs and environments will be included in future updates.  If you like music and exercise games do yourself a favor and check this game out!

developer_response.body: Hey hollywoodhammer, thanks for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We're happy to hear that the core mechanics of the game are coming across as fun and unique to you! More things are being worked on behind the scenes as we continue to expand the game and we appreciate your enthusiasm, support, and recommendation for Peak Rhythm!

reviews.review_description: I absolutely love the concept here, but I struggled to keep up as the game difficulty ramped up after the first few difficulty ratings. So ymmv on how far in love you fall for this game depending on how well you handle the more advanced techniques and speed needed for the more advanced levels.

developer_response.body:
Thanks for the 4 stars! We know Difficulty 4+ jumps and twists are a big step up, but they’re high-reward once you find the rhythm.

We’re working on single-player tempo controls and more lower-diff maps for a future update to help with the learning curve. We’d love to have you in our Discord to hear more of your feedback.

reviews.review_description: Peak rhythm is an awesome music game with a workout within  the game . The music is on point with the graphics and visuals,  the graphics are superb. I love that the music drives you to go further and  further.  Here are some reasons why I like this game, it's single user,  they have a multiplayer climb where you can climb with your friends, there is voice chat , you can   customize your avatar any way you want the possibilities are endless. All in all I would recommend this game to anyone who loves music and getting down to the beat .

developer_response.body: Hey skyvuc, thanks for your recommendation! It's wonderful to hear that Peak Rhythm is checking so many of your boxes. Right now, we're working on expanding the game further, so stay tuned and thanks again for playing during our Early Access launch!

reviews.review_title: Feel the Rhythm Feel The Climb
reviews.review_description:
Rhythm games are some of my favorite  games out there. VR introduced a new genre to gaming with climbing, and Flat2VR Studios has brought a combination of both those genres into something very fun and exciting. 

Climbing to a beat might sound like there isn't much else, but the added gameplay mechanics and even the drop feels like this game has plenty to keep it going in Early Access. 

With a newly developed custom map editor, it allows for so much variety that your imagination becomes the only roadblock. Check out Peak Rhythm for some really fun and interesting experiences only VR can provide.

reviews.review_title: Plays Like A VR Rock Wall Rhythm Game
reviews.review_description: Peak Rhythm feels like a rhythm game set against a rock wall.  There are multiple moves you can do with the hand holds (grab, twist, drop, jump) that add to the fun gameplay.  The visual and audio cues are really good too, so it didn't take me long at all to get to the point where I was just fluidly navigating through the levels without even having to really think about what I was doing.  While the music is good I'd like to see more music genres added to the game as well as more environments to play in, but other than that I had a blast playing it.

developer_response.body:
Thanks so much for the review and for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! It’s great to hear that the movement, visual cues, and audio cues came together to let you get into that smooth, flow-state feeling so quickly. That sense of fluid rhythm and movement is exactly what we’re aiming for.

We also appreciate your feedback on the variety of music and the environments. We have more songs in development and plan to collaborate with other labels and artists to diversify the genres over time. Expanding the game’s visual variety is also something we aim to build on throughout Early Access.

reviews.review_title: Where Every Beat Lifts You Higher
reviews.review_description:
Peak Rhythm surprised me with how physical it feels. After a few minutes, I wasn’t just pressing buttons—I was moving with the music. Grabbing holds on the 5×5 grid and nailing Multi-Twists felt satisfying, especially when perfect timing launched me upward through the glowing city. The electronic soundtrack hits hard, and matching my movements to the beat made runs feel intense and focused.

The climbing mechanics are smart and rewarding, but they can be tiring during longer sessions, and missing notes can feel punishing while learning new tracks. Content is a bit limited right now, though the Beatmap Editor adds real value and replayability.

Overall, Peak Rhythm is stylish, challenging, and different from most rhythm games. With planned updates, it has strong potential.

Peak Rhythm is an interesting and quirky mix of genres. You play as a cliff-climbing character using anchors to scale a wall, but the twist is that to achieve the best scores you must also follow the rhythm and build combos in time with the music.

As the song progresses, you gradually learn new mechanics such as twists, drops, and other climbing techniques that keep the gameplay evolving. The execution is spotless, with very polished mechanics that make the experience feel both precise and satisfying.

The game is currently in Early Access, but the studio is actively working on a multiplayer mode along with a few other surprises. The visuals are deliberately sober and minimalist, which works well since the real focus of the game is the rhythm-based climbing.

A unique concept that blends climbing and rhythm gameplay in a very original way. 🎶🧗‍♀️

Combining climbing (and other actions) with rhythm timing was fun. The music fits the game well, but it'd be great if customizable music options were added. It's pretty great as it is, though. It can get very addicting with a "I can surely do better than that" type of feel."

A very solid game, reminiscent of OSU. Currently, there aren't many songs, but the ones it has are very well made. The grab has a very nice snap, and the twist is satisfying. For me, dropping was very difficult to get right, so practice is definitely needed for the harder difficulties. Overall, good visuals, good audio, and good movement. Worth a try.

developer_response.body: Thanks for the review and for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! It’s great to hear the core feel of the game is landing for you, from the movement and timing to the visuals and audio. Drops and Jumps are definitely mechanics that take practice, but we’re also working to smooth the learning curve during Early Access with more lower-difficulty maps. We know the current song count is still limited, but we already have more songs in development and look forward to adding them as soon as they’re ready.

reviews.review_description: It's a fun and unique combined use of the beat/music and climbing genres. A worthwhile addition to any VR music players headset.

Easy to learn, difficult to master, higher difficulties get very frantic and challenging. Included music is lively and fun to work out to. 

Initial levels consist of a simple grab mechanic that is very intuitive but later levels start throwing more complex mechanics.

developer_response.body: Hey Gr1llMeACheese, thanks for the review and for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch!

reviews.review_description: This game was definitely fun playing. All thought it can be challenging at times but the music did make the game fun when playing. I do look forward to seeing more levels and future content. I definitely recommend to those who need a good workout.

developer_response.body: Thanks for the 5-star review and for playing Peak Rhythm during Early Access! We’re glad you had fun and that the music made the challenging moments enjoyable. We look forward to expanding the game with more levels and content. Thanks again for your support and recommendation!

reviews.review_title: Interesting idea. Just not for me
reviews.review_description: The game is pretty and stuff but it just didn't grab me sorry guys

developer_response.body: Hey TipsySonic, no problem at all. Thank you for giving us a shot!

reviews.review_description: fun!

This game is great ! Starts with easy rythm climbing, you then find harder difficulties with jumps, twisting ;)
Have fun playing with your friends ;)

developer_response.body: Thanks for the review and for playing Peak Rhythm during our Early Access launch! We’re glad you’re enjoying the progression from easier rhythm-climbing maps to more advanced mechanics as the difficulty increases. That sense of growth and variety is a big part of our goal. We also appreciate the mention of playing with friends and look forward to building on that with our upcoming Multiplayer Update!

reviews.review_description: So far, I haven’t experienced anything that’s truly unique or extoarsinary about this game. Calling it a rythm game is by the bare minimum requirement. You’re basically rock climbing but the “rythm” part is youre guided by a colored path and you have to grab a the right moment when it reaches the designated circular orb. The “rythm” part feels more like just well timing than actually following the timing of the music . Yes you can make it more challenging with different paths but I dunno. I was hoping for something unique in terms of mechanics/disign, etc. you’re just on this giant digital grid like wall. Graphics and music choose idols but not really noteworthy. I guess this could be for a more casual rock climbing enthusiasts that wants some beats to accompany their experience. But unfortunately I personally don’t find this stimulating enough.

developer_response.body:
Hi! Thanks for grabbing Peak Rhythm on launch day and taking the time to share your thoughts. Since we’re in Early Access, feedback like yours really helps us improve the game.

It seems you’ve been playing it as a climbing sim with music, but our main goal is to create a high-energy rhythm-action game. Imagine DDR, but with vertical movement instead of horizontal. The digital grid helps you focus on the notes, and the platform speed changes based on how well you play. Your height will matter even more in our upcoming Multiplayer Update, where you’ll race other players to the top.

We recommend trying some of the Difficulty 2+ maps. That’s where our mappers let the choreography shine, incorporating twist notes and jump combinations that sync with the song’s intensity. If you aim for those “Perfect” hits instead of just “Hits,” you’ll start to feel the physical flow we’re aiming for!

We’d love to hear more of your thoughts on how we can make the rhythm feel even more impactful. Feel free to join our official Discord to chat with us directly!
